phrasalVerbs.definition: To think of something such as an idea or a plan.
examples: She came up with a brilliant solution to the problem.
Can you come up with any ideas for the party?
We need to come up with a new strategy.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To be able to understand something or solve a problem; to understand what someone is like and why they behave in a certain way.
examples: I can't figure out how this machine works.
It took me hours to figure out the puzzle.
She finally figured out why he was upset.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make a mistake or do something badly; to make something dirty or untidy.
examples: I really messed up the job interview.
Don't mess up my room—I just cleaned it!
He messed up the recipe by adding too much salt.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To put things together without any order; to think that one person or thing is another.
examples: I always mix up their names because they're twins.
She mixed up the files on her desk.
Don't mix up the ingredients before measuring them.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To consider a problem or decision carefully.
examples: I need time to think over your offer.
Think it over before you make a final decision.
She thought over the situation before responding.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ ___ ___ a plan for the project.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come up with' means to think of an idea or plan.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you help me ___ out how to use this software?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Figure out' means to understand or solve something.

fillInExercises.sentence: I completely ___ ___ the presentation. I forgot half of it.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Mess up' means to make a mistake or do something badly.

fillInExercises.sentence: I always ___ up the twins because they look so similar.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Mix up' means to confuse one person or thing with another.

fillInExercises.sentence: Let me ___ ___ your proposal before I give you an answer.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Think over' means to consider something carefully.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ up with a creative solution to the budget problem.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come up with' means to produce an idea or solution.

fillInExercises.sentence: It took me ages to ___ ___ the answer to the riddle.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Figure out' means to solve or understand something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't ___ up the paperwork—keep everything organised.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Mess up' can mean to make something untidy or disorganised.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: We have a big issue with the budget. Can you help me [figure out] what went wrong?

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Sure. Let me [think over] the numbers first.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: I think someone [mess up] the calculations.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Actually, I [mix up] two of the spreadsheets yesterday. That might be it.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: No worries. Let's [come up with] a way to fix it.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: I'll [figure out] the correct totals by this afternoon.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Have you [think over] which job offer to accept?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Not yet. I can't [figure out] which one is better for me.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Don't worry. You won't [mess up] this decision if you take your time.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: I just don't want to [mix up] my priorities.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Maybe you can [come up with] a list of pros and cons?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: That's a good idea. Let me [think over] it this weekend.
